event: Saul consults the spirit of Samuel through the Witch of Endor
symbols: shrouded ghost of Samuel — the dead prophet raised against divine law, bearing God's final judgment on Saul
skeleton and demonic figures above — the realm of death and the demonic powers associated with necromancy
Saul prostrating himself — the king's total spiritual collapse and desperate submission to forbidden powers
the witch's commanding gesture — the medium's power over the dark ritual, contrasting with God's silence toward Saul
armor and fallen military equipment — Saul's royal authority now useless, his earthly power unable to save him
soldiers hiding at left — Saul's companions cowering in fear, witnessing the king's disgrace
darkness pervading the scene — spiritual blindness and the absence of God's guiding light
description: An engraving after Salvator Rosa depicting the dramatic scene of Saul consulting the ghost of Samuel through the Witch of Endor. At center, the shrouded figure of Samuel's spirit stands upright, wrapped in white burial garments, confronting the terrified Saul who reaches toward the apparition. The witch stands between them, gesturing dramatically. Above the scene, a chaotic assembly of supernatural figures — skeletons, demons, and spectral beings — swirl in the darkness, representing the demonic realm invoked by the necromantic ritual. At left, Saul's frightened companions cower behind rocks.
theological_description: The account of Saul's visit to the medium at Endor (1 Sam 28) represents one of the darkest episodes in Israel's monarchical history. Saul, having been rejected by God for his repeated disobedience, finds himself facing the Philistine army with no divine guidance: 'When Saul inquired of the LORD, the LORD did not answer him, either by dreams or by Urim or by prophets' (1 Sam 28:6). In desperation, the king who had himself banned mediums and necromancers from Israel (1 Sam 28:3) now secretly seeks one out, violating the very law he had enforced. The Mosaic Law explicitly forbids consulting the dead: 'There shall not be found among you anyone who practices divination or tells fortunes or interprets omens, or a sorcerer or a charmer or a medium or a necromancer or one who inquires of the dead' (Deut 18:10-11). The ghostly Samuel delivers no comfort but rather God's final judgment: 'The LORD has torn the kingdom out of your hand and given it to your neighbor, David... Tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me' (1 Sam 28:17-19). This scene dramatizes the catastrophic end of a king who began with promise but whose persistent disobedience led to complete spiritual abandonment. The demonic figures hovering above in Rosa's composition reflect the theological reality that necromancy opens a door to the powers of darkness.

scripture_refs.reason: The full account of Saul's visit to the Witch of Endor and the raising of Samuel's spirit
God's silence toward Saul — the divine abandonment that drives him to seek a medium
Samuel's ghost delivers God's irrevocable judgment: the kingdom is torn away and Saul will die the next day
The Mosaic prohibition against necromancy and divination that Saul violates in this scene
The Chronicler's summary judgment: Saul died for his unfaithfulness, including consulting a medium instead of the LORD
Samuel's earlier warning that rebellion is as the sin of divination — Saul's disobedience has now literally led him to divination

_meta.title: Saul Consults Samuel After the Witch of Endor Has Raised Him
